question: Do PHIL-26 predictions P3 (diminishing compaction returns) and P4 (human signals break fixed points) hold?
method: P3: analyze 18 compaction rounds from proxy-k-log.json (62 entries, S74-S510). Test monotonic decrease. P4: correlate 86 human signals (9 key) with post-signal lesson production, novelty rate, domain dispatch from SESSION-LOG.md.
prediction: P3: returns DO diminish (PHIL-26 claims NP landscape saturation). P4: escapes correlate with signals (PHIL-26 claims oracle access).
falsification: P3 falsified if: later rounds save >= earlier rounds. P4 falsified if: post-signal novelty <= baseline.

P3_compaction_returns.mechanism: Returns are opportunity-bounded (depend on accumulated compactable material), not round-bounded. Scale effect: larger systems have more targets.

P4_fixed_point_escapes.limitations: small key-signal sample, post-hoc window (1-5 sessions), concurrency confound at N>=5

verdict: PHIL-26 meets DROP criterion. 2/4 predictions falsified (P1: lessons increase with N, P3: compaction returns increase not decrease). P4 supported but does not require NP framing. NP metaphor is descriptively interesting but operationally inert — 0 tools, 0 decisions in 25 sessions.
